content:
# Romans Chapter 14 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 14 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 14:1 — "welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions" vs. "don't argue with them about what they think is right or wrong"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And the [one] being weak in the faith receive, not for judgments of reasonings." | "Judgments of reasonings" for διακρίσεις διαλογισμῶν — extremely literal |
| LOGOS | "As for the one who is weak in faith, welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions." | "Quarrel over opinions" — idiomatic; "welcome" for προσλαμβάνεσθε |
| PNEUMA | "Accept other believers who are weak in faith, and don't argue with them about what they think is right or wrong." | "Accept" for προσλαμβάνεσθε; "argue... about what they think is right or wrong" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "judgments of reasonings" is opaque. LOGOS's "quarrel over opinions" captures the sense of διακρίσεις διαλογισμῶν (not to pass judgment on disputed matters). PNEUMA's expansion is accurate but wordy.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:4 — "the servant of another" vs. "someone else's servants"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Who are you, the [one] judging another's household servant? To the own lord he stands or falls." | "Household servant" for οἰκέτην — specific term (domestic slave) |
| LOGOS | "Who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master that he stands or falls." | "Servant" for οἰκέτην; "master" for κυρίῳ |
| PNEUMA | "Who are you to condemn someone else's servants? Their own master will judge whether they stand or fall." | Pluralizes; "condemn" for κρίνων; "judge" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "household servant" is more precise for οἰκέτης (a domestic servant as opposed to δοῦλος), but the distinction does not carry in English context. PNEUMA changes the singular to plural and substitutes "condemn" for "judge" (κρίνω), which is a stronger word than Paul uses here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:5 — "esteems one day as better than another" vs. "thinks one day is more holy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "One judges [one] day above [another] day; another judges every day [alike]." | "Judges" for κρίνει — literal (same root as "judge") |
| LOGOS | "One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike." | "Esteems" for κρίνει — more appropriate for evaluative sense |
| PNEUMA | "some think one day is more holy than another day, while others think every day is alike." | "More holy" — adds "holy" (not in Greek); pluralizes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "holy" to "more holy," which interprets the passage specifically as about sacred days. While this is likely the context, Paul leaves it as a general comparative. LOGOS's "esteems" appropriately captures the evaluative sense of κρίνω here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:9 — "Christ died and lived again" vs. "Christ died and rose again"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For to this [end] Christos died and lived, that he might be Lord of both [the] dead and [the] living." | "Died and lived" — ἀπέθανεν καὶ ἔζησεν; literal |
| LOGOS | "For to this end Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living." | "Lived again" — adds "again" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "Christ died and rose again for this very purpose — to be Lord both of the living and of the dead." | "Rose again" — interprets ἔζησεν as resurrection specifically; reverses "living/dead" order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek ἔζησεν ("lived/came to life") is not the standard resurrection verb (ἠγέρθη), though it refers to the same event. LOGOS's "lived again" preserves Paul's word choice. PNEUMA's "rose again" substitutes the standard resurrection vocabulary, and reverses the order of "dead and living" to "living and dead" (against the Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Paul's word choice "lived again" preserved.

---

## 14:10 — "judgment seat of God" — textual variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we will all stand before the judgment seat of the God" | "Of the God" — follows Sinaiticus reading τοῦ θεοῦ |
| LOGOS | "we will all stand before the judgment seat of God" | Same reading, without article |
| PNEUMA | "Remember, we will all stand before the judgment seat of God." | Adds "Remember" — not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three follow the Sinaiticus reading (θεοῦ rather than Χριστοῦ). Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "Remember" as a conversational filler.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:14 — "unclean" vs. "common" vs. "wrong to eat"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "nothing [is] common through itself; except to the [one] reckoning anything to be common, to that [one it is] common" | "Common" for κοινόν — the Jewish ritual purity term (profane, not set apart) |
| LOGOS | "nothing is unclean in itself, but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean" | "Unclean" for κοινόν — communicates the purity concept |
| PNEUMA | "no food, in and of itself, is wrong to eat. But if someone believes it is wrong, then for that person it is wrong." | "Wrong to eat" — moral rather than ritual language; adds "food" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "unclean." The Jewish purity concept (κοινός = "common" in the sense of ritually profane) is best rendered as "unclean" for English readers familiar with biblical purity language. EMET's "common" preserves the etymology but is misleading in modern English ("common" now means "ordinary"). PNEUMA's "wrong to eat" shifts from ritual purity to moral categories.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Unclean" captures the ritual sense.

---

## 14:15 — "destroy the one for whom Christ died" vs. "ruin someone for whom Christ died"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not by the food of you destroy that [one] for whom Christos died." | "Destroy" for ἀπόλλυε |
| LOGOS | "By what you eat, do not destroy the one for whom Christ died." | "Destroy" — same verb |
| PNEUMA | "Don't let your eating ruin someone for whom Christ died." | "Ruin" — slightly softer; "someone" generalizes |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three are close; the key choice is between "destroy" (EMET/LOGOS) and "ruin" (PNEUMA) for ἀπόλλυμι. "Destroy" is the stronger and more accurate rendering of this verb (which can mean "destroy, lose, perish"), and its severity is Paul's rhetorical point — careless exercise of freedom can spiritually devastate a fellow believer.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Destroy" retained for full rhetorical force.

---

## 14:17 — "righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "righteousness and peace and joy in [the] Holy Spirit" | Standard |
| LOGOS | "righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit" | Standard |
| PNEUMA | "living a life of goodness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit" | Changes "righteousness" to "living a life of goodness" — paraphrase; changes lexical category |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA changes the noun "righteousness" (δικαιοσύνη) to a verbal phrase "living a life of goodness," which loses the specific Pauline term that has been central to the entire letter. "Righteousness" must be preserved for thematic coherence with Romans 1-8.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET. "Righteousness" retained.

---

## 14:20 — "destroy the work of God" vs. "tear apart the work of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not for the sake of food tear down the work of the God." | "Tear down" for κατάλυε |
| LOGOS | "Do not, for the sake of food, destroy the work of God." | "Destroy" for κατάλυε |
| PNEUMA | "Don't tear apart the work of God over what you eat." | "Tear apart" — more vivid; "over what you eat" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. κατάλυε means "tear down, destroy, demolish." EMET's "tear down" and LOGOS's "destroy" are both accurate. PNEUMA's "tear apart" adds a different image (ripping rather than demolishing). LOGOS's "destroy" is clearest.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:22 — "Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Blessed [is] the [one] not judging himself in what he approves." | Concise; literal |
| LOGOS | "Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves." | Expanded; adds "has no reason" |
| PNEUMA | "Blessed are those who don't feel guilty for doing something they have decided is right." | "Don't feel guilty" — psychological language; "decided is right" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "don't feel guilty" psychologizes what Paul frames in judicial terms (κρίνω — "judge," not "feel guilty"). The macarism is about the person whose conscience does not condemn their own choices.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:23 — "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin" vs. "If you do anything you believe is not right, you are sinning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And everything which [is] not from faith is sin." | "From faith" for ἐκ πίστεως — compact maxim |
| LOGOS | "For whatever does not proceed from faith is sin." | "Proceed from faith" — formal; preserves maxim form |
| PNEUMA | "If you do anything you believe is not right, you are sinning." | Conditional sentence; "believe is not right" narrows πίστις to personal conviction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This verse functions as a maxim (general principle), and its lapidary quality should be preserved. PNEUMA's conditional sentence turns a principle into a specific scenario. The term πίστις here means "faith/conviction" — PNEUMA's "believe is not right" narrows it to subjective belief.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The maxim form preserved.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA shifts from ritual/theological to moral/psychological language | vv.5, 14, 17, 22 | "Holy" for evaluative; "wrong" for unclean; "goodness" for righteousness; "feel guilty" for judge |
| PNEUMA adds conversational fillers | vv.10, 14 | "Remember"; "food, in and of itself" |
| EMET's technical vocabulary vs. English readability | vv.1, 4, 14 | "Judgments of reasonings"; "household servant"; "common" |
| Preserving maxim/proverb form | v.23 | LOGOS/EMET preserve; PNEUMA converts to conditional |
| Verb precision for ἀπόλλυμι and κατάλυε | vv.15, 20 | "Destroy" vs. "ruin" vs. "tear apart" |
| Theological term consistency with rest of Romans | v.17 | "Righteousness" must be preserved for letter coherence |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 14 addresses practical community ethics (food, holy days, mutual acceptance), and the translations diverge most sharply on the relationship between ritual and moral language. PNEUMA consistently converts Paul's ritual purity vocabulary into modern moral categories ("unclean" becomes "wrong to eat," "righteousness" becomes "goodness"), which is pastorally accessible but loses the specific Jewish-Christian context Paul is addressing. The chapter also contains key proverbial statements (v.8, v.17, v.23) that function as memorable maxims — LOGOS preserves their compact form while PNEUMA expands them into conversational sentences. EMET provides the most literal renderings but at the cost of English clarity (especially v.1's "judgments of reasonings"). SOPHIA consistently chose LOGOS's balanced approach, preserving both theological vocabulary and readability.

---
